rationale: The citing paper uses Bolton and Reed (2004) in its theory section for Experiment 2b to support the claim that identity-based judgments are resistant to counterpersuasion. That claim is then used to justify the mechanism behind a key moderator prediction, namely that identity-congruent donor cues should be more influential when people are focused on others. This is more than a passing bibliography mention because it informs the paper's conceptual setup for hypothesis development.
relation_description: The citing study draws on Sticky Priors as conceptual evidence that identity-based judgments can persist despite corrective pressure. It uses this premise to motivate why identity-congruent social information should shape donation decisions, especially under other-focused attention.
